text: in this video we describe our tips and tricks for the realization of total laparoscopic hysterectomy in a young patient with the previous three scar section it's a young patient so we decided to conserve both adnexa and for that we have to cut the round ligament the tube and the utero-ovarian ligament one centimeter outside their uterine insertion it's important to control the adnexal vein which is sometimes closer than we think as you see we have after to open the brow ligament

The surgeon continues dissection toward the left uterosacral ligament, cutting the posterior lip of the broad ligament to increase the distance between the ureter and the left uterine artery. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side, cutting the round ligament one centimeter outside the cornea, followed by cutting the tube and the utero-ovarian ligament.

The assistant uses scissors perpendicular to the tissue to detach the adnexa from the uterine attachment. The surgeon then begins to skeletonize the uterine artery and develop the posterior lip of the brown ligament toward the right uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon begins a complex vesicovaginal dissection, with assistants retracting the bladder. The dissection starts laterally, with small bites of tissue being cut to locate the optimal dissecting point.

The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully cutting through scar tissue under visual control. The bladder is filled with methylene blue to check for any bladder injury, while the assistant holds the bladder cranially. The vesicovaginal plane becomes recognizable after filling the bladder.

The surgeon attempts to individualize the Halban fascia, pulling down the bladder toward the vagina. The medial aspect of the bladder pillar is cut, revealing dense scar tissue. Coagulation is applied to each tissue segment to control bleeding.

The surgeon fills the bladder with methylene blue to check for potential injuries, then continues the vesicovaginal dissection until reaching the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The medial part of the right bladder pillar is cut, revealing tissue response. The uterine artery and vein are controlled after coagulation.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy using a monocular needle, cutting the anterior aspect of the vagina, the left and right lateral aspects, and the upper parts of the uterosacral ligaments. The posterior aspect of the vagina is also incised, facilitating the safe removal of the uterus via the vaginal route. The bladder remains visibly filled with methylene blue.

The surgeon performs hemostasis on the vagina using stitches, starting with the first stitch placed inside the uterosacral ligament. The vaginal mucosa is included in the stitch to reduce the risk of post-operative bleeding. The vagina is closed using water knots, which are auto-blocking, and interrupted sutures are preferred over running sutures.
The surgeon uses a right wheel hole stitch with a 14mm needle to close the final knot, ensuring the vagina is completely closed with a tupple cut technique.
So this is our technique for total laparoscopic hysterectomy in young patients. And thank you for attention.
